evidence: Multiple files define separate large `*Runtime` seam structs (e.g. `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go`, `goseg/routines/runtime_seams.go`, `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`) with many function fields and separate `WithRuntime` wrappers, while the underlying behavior remains coupled to package-level state.
Several constructors follow a “default + with-runtime” pattern that mostly delegates through wrappers rather than introducing true abstraction boundaries.

`goseg/importer/importer.go` and `goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go` accept runtime/config-like inputs, but key operations still reference ambient package values and helper globals, so seam injection is only partial.

leak tests in this batch validate helper paths (`TestUpdateBroadcastSendsFullAndScopedPayloads`, `TestProcessActionForwardsDefaultActions`) with pre-seeded maps/channels, but no concurrent listener lifecycle path is exercised.

`listener` mutates and deletes entries in `BytesChan`/`lickStatuses` under lock, while production update paths read those maps without lock (`updateBroadcast`, `urbitLogin`), yet listed tests do not run a live `listener` goroutine with teardown overlap.
The current test set has no race-oriented or teardown/race regression coverage for `updateBroadcast` while a listener is closing and removing the per-ship channel map entry.
`listener` updates `BytesChan[patp]` and `lickStatuses[patp]` while holding `lickMu`, but `updateBroadcast` and `urbitLogin` access `BytesChan` without synchronization and can run concurrently.
`GetLickStatuses` returns the internal `lickStatuses` map directly, and callers iterate or index it outside any lock in `handler.go` and `broadcast.go`.
`PublishSchedulePack` performs a direct send on an unbuffered `schedulePackBus` and `PackScheduleLoop` is the only consumer.
In `Shipworkflow.schedulePack`, `broadcast.PublishSchedulePack("schedule")` is called directly on the API/request path, so caller latency can be coupled to receiver availability.

findings.summary: Runtime/context seams are not honored consistently, so tests can override behavior without preventing runtime dependence on global state.
evidence: Runtime initialization in `importer` exposes overridable paths/ops, but the implementation still relies on package-level config state in the default path/IO path construction path, so overrides cannot fully isolate behavior.

`RuntimeContextSnapshot` is introduced, yet callers in multiple modules still pull from package globals (for example base/path/runtime flags) instead of consistently threading the injected snapshot through seams.
findings.suggestion: Define and propagate one canonical context object per orchestration path (no fallback reads from package globals). Replace direct `config`/global reads with context-sourced accessors inside runtime seam ops, and enforce via compilation checks/tests that runtime entrypoints only consume fields from the injected runtime struct.

findings.summary: Operation functions are wrapped in repetitive `WithRuntime` pass-through chains that add indirection without adding abstraction value.
evidence: Each file exposes base implementations plus `WithRuntime` variants that forward calls with minimal or no added logic.
The same delegation pattern appears across these orchestration modules, creating deeper call stacks and repeated seams for logically equivalent behavior.
findings.suggestion: Collapse duplicated `WithRuntime` wrappers into a single seam entrypoint per bounded operation and move the injectability boundary to the package edge; keep test seams at one layer instead of stacked forwarders.

findings.summary: Runtime seam structs aggregate too many unrelated dependencies, reducing interface honesty and making call sites harder to reason about.
evidence: Each seam type combines numerous helper operations (process/env/os/config/logging/IO helpers) into one broad struct, making dependencies coarse-grained and hiding true component boundaries.
Consumers frequently receive only a subset of these services but are forced to accept the entire bag, indicating low abstraction precision.
findings.suggestion: Split seam definitions into narrower operation-specific interfaces (for example split lifecycle, network, filesystem, and config concerns) and inject only the minimal seam set needed per module.

findings.summary: Shared leak maps are read and written without synchronization across goroutines
evidence: `listener` mutates `BytesChan[patp] = make(chan string)` and deletes it while holding `lickMu`.
`updateBroadcast` reads `channel, exists := BytesChan[patp]` with no lock and sends to that channel.
`urbitLogin` sends on `BytesChan[patp]` with no lock, and the listener goroutine also receives via `case broadcast := <-BytesChan[patp]`.
`GetLickStatuses` returns `lickStatuses` by reference; callers (`processAction`, `handler`) iterate/index map content without any lock boundary.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a dedicated lock for `BytesChan` access and never expose mutable maps directly; take a local snapshot (`chan string, ok`) under lock before sending/receiving, and always return a copied `lickStatuses` map from `GetLickStatuses`.

findings.summary: Leak broadcast concurrency races are not covered by tests despite shared mutable state
evidence: Tests in this batch call `updateBroadcast`/`processAction` against static maps and channels, not a live listener lifecycle.
No listed test drives concurrent `listener` startup/teardown while `updateBroadcast` sends or `urbitLogin` writes to `BytesChan`.
The same files under test include unsynchronized shared map access that can produce panics or races under concurrent close/send patterns, but those scenarios are untested.
findings.suggestion: Add race-focused tests that run an actual `listener` goroutine, trigger concurrent `updateBroadcast` and `urbitLogin` while closing/removing a ship channel, and assert no map iteration/send panics plus clean channel shutdown.

findings.summary: Unbuffered schedule bus couples API path to background loop availability
evidence: `schedulePackBus` is declared as an unbuffered channel, and `PublishSchedulePack` performs a direct blocking send.
`schedulePack` in shipworkflow calls `broadcast.PublishSchedulePack("schedule")` synchronously in the request/control path.
The consumer is `PackScheduleLoop`, which only runs in a background goroutine and can be absent or temporarily delayed; there is no fallback path when receiver is unavailable.
findings.suggestion: Use a buffered channel or non-blocking select in `PublishSchedulePack`, and add a drop/metric behavior for overflow or no-receiver windows; optionally switch to a direct scheduler callback if order is not required.
